coredata.dc:title: Superlubricity achieved by carbon quantum dots in ionic liquid
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Wei
Gong, Zhenbin
Gao, Kaixiong
Qiang, Li
Zhang, Junyan
Yu, Shurong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and ionic liquid (IL) nanoparticles were fabricated by simple modification. The structures and properties of these particles were measured with HRTEM, FTIR, Raman Spectrometer and friction test. More importantly, IL-modified CQDs as lubricant material demonstrated excellent tribological performance and obtained a super-low friction coefficient about 0.006 and wear rate about 0.7×10−14 m3/Nm with the CQDs content of 3.6%. The results revealed that IL-modified CQDs might provide a new idea to investigate various lubricant materials to achieve super-low friction and could be intensively studied for other practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs nanoparticles
Ionic liquid
IL-modified CQDs
Superlubricity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of the assembling process by IL- modified CQDs.

(a) The TEM micrograph of the IL-CQDs; (b) particles size distribution; (c) selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern; (d) FTIR spectra of IL and IL-CQDs; (e) Raman spectra of IL/CQDs with the different CDs content.

Friction coefficient curves as a function of the sliding cycle with CQDs content by (a) Al2O3 ball and (b) steel ball. (c) The 2D cross-section surface profile of wear tracks with CQDs content; (d) the wear rate of IL-CQDs as a function of the CQDs content with two contact balls.

head.ce:title: Superlubricity achieved by carbon quantum dots in ionic liquid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Petrochemical Engineering, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou 730050, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and ionic liquid (IL) nanoparticles were fabricated by simple modification. The structures and properties of these particles were measured with HRTEM, FTIR, Raman Spectrometer and friction test. More importantly, IL-modified CQDs as lubricant material demonstrated excellent tribological performance and obtained a super-low friction coefficient about 0.006 and wear rate about 0.7×10−14 m3/Nm with the CQDs content of 3.6%. The results revealed that IL-modified CQDs might provide a new idea to investigate various lubricant materials to achieve super-low friction and could be intensively studied for other practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction and wear is a multi-scale phenomenon which exists from atomic scale to galaxy scale, such as vascular injury [1], earthquake [2], volcanic eruptions [3], and even galaxy collisions [4]. With the rapidly development of mechanical systems, friction and wear became a core issue, which resulting in significant energy loss, resource consumption and environmental pollution [5]. Macro/microscopic superlubricity are effective methods to solve the negative effect of friction and wear. As the decrease of device scale, it is essential to pay much attention to reduce friction and wear in micro-scale. Until now, outstanding achievements were obtained in micro-scale superlubricity [6,7]. However, the superlubricity achieved by reducing of Van der Waals force, surface force, adhesion and friction [8–10] usually need rigorous conditions, such as high vacuum, high temperature and specific materials [11,12]. Thus, microscopic superlubricity is still difficult to apply in the practical working condition.

Water-soluble lubricants and IL modified nanomaterials (such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and fullerene) have been extensively investigated and showed the better tribological performance by reasonable design of the core-shell structure and ordered charge distribution [13]. It could provide a way to improve the tribological properties of nano-materials with composite structures, which formed through interaction of nanoparticles with polar molecules. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which have intrinsic characteristics such as quasi-spherical, the high surface area and size below 10nm [14], could potentially be better than traditional nanoparticles when modified with IL, and the tribological or mechanical properties of related researches on IL modified CQDs are still in its infancy [15–17]. Herein, we designed and fabricated novel nanoparticles by IL-modified CQDs, which reveals excellent friction-reduction and anti-wear performance and might provide a new idea to investigate various lubricant materials.

The CQDs used in the experiment were synthesized by a one step acid assisted ultrasonic treatment from glucose and HCl [18]. The used IL was mixture solution of choline chloride and ethylene glycol (molar ratio 1:2) [19]. The IL/CQDs were attained by grinding and finally dropped into the clear plastic tubes.

The silicon (111) chips used were ultrasonically pre-cleaned and then modified in the IL/CQDs (CQDs content was 0%, 0.98%, 3.6%, 8.2%) for 48h. The structure of IL/CQDs was characterized by TEM (TF 20, FEI, USA), FTIR (IFS 66 V/S, Bruker, GER) and Raman spectroscopy (HR 800, JBY, Japan). The tribological behavior of the IL/CQDs nanoparticles was evaluated on the rotating ball-on-disc tester. Steel and Al2O3 balls (φ=3nm) were used as counterparts, with a rotating speed of 200 r/min, and the applied load was 20mN. The wear volume was estimated by Micro-XAM 3D surface profiler (MicroMAXTM model, ADE Phase Shift, USA). The experiment was carried out under atmosphere condition with the relative humidity of 30%.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the IL is randomly distributed on the surface of CQDs in the first period. Subsequently, the IL is orderly distributed on the CQDs surface owing to interaction of the CQDs with IL, also including the high specific surface area of the CQDs and the high density polar functional groups of the IL. Finally, similar to preparing water-soluble and IL-modified CNTs [20–22], we speculate that the sphere-in-shell structures are formed owing to the charge effect and the shape of the CQDs (as the core) and IL (as the shell).

Fig.2 (a) demonstrates the TEM micrograph of the as-prepared near spherical morphology IL-CQDs, which have excellent dispersibility and dense granular. The average diameter of IL-CQDs particles is about 5.6nm as shown in size distribution diagram (Fig.2b). Fig.2(c) shows a broad and diffuse halo with two diffraction rings (from inner to outer) correspond to about spacing of 2.13Å and 1.12Å, which are consistent with that of (100) and (006) facet of graphite. Apparently, the above results indicate that the as-prepared CQDs are graphite nanocrystals through modified by IL.

The structural of the IL-CQDs were obtained by the FTIR spectrum, as shown in Fig.2(d). The C–H stretching vibration band occurs at 2360cm−1 after modification. The peak at 1706cm−1 and 1728cm−1 is associated with the CO stretching vibration bands, whereas the peak at 1450cm−1 is attributed to the absorption peaks of –COO− [23]. These data show there are –COOH groups on the CQDs surface. The stretching vibration bands of C–O at 1109cm−1 indicatethe presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, and no characteristic absorption peaks of the IL are found [24]. The FTIR data confirm IL molecular were attached on the CQDs surface and no change in peak position until 3.6% CQDs content.

Fig.2(e) illustrates Raman spectra of IL-CQDs. It shows normal Raman features for CQDs, the G peak at 1580cm−1 attributes to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms, while the D peak at 1360cm−1 is related to the presence of sp3 carbon atoms defects [25]. The R value (area ratio of Da/Ga) of IL-modified CQDs increased to 1.3 and compared the R value of pristine CQDs is 1.1. The results demonstrates the defect of CQDs increased during functionalization, which might result in the position shift of D and G peak, such as the presence of carboxylic group at 1450cm−1 of G peak. The Raman studies are consistent well with the FTIR results and ensure that more IL molecular be attached onto the CQDs surface.

As shown in Fig.3 (a), the friction coefficient for the silicon wafer is 0.28 and the serve fluctuating of friction coefficient curve is also observed for the silicon with pure IL. The depth of the wear tracks is about 110nm as revealed from Fig.3(c) and the wear rate is 4.8×10−14 m3/Nm (Fig.3(d)). When the CQDs content increases to 0.98%, the friction coefficient decreases to 0.12, and the wear depth decreases to about 80nm and the wear rate decreases to 2.5×10−14 m3/Nm (Fig.3(c) and (d)). As the CQDs content increases to 3.6%, the lowest friction coefficient of about 0.006 could be observed. The wear depth further declines to about 38nm and the wear rate decrease to the lowest value (0.7×10−14 m3/Nm). While, when the CQDs content increases to 8.2%, the friction coefficient increases to about 0.05, and the wear depth and wear rate increase to about 55nm and 1.9×10−14 m3/Nm, respectively. The friction coefficients shown in Fig.3(b) are correspondingly higher than that shown in Fig.3(a) due to the more serious corrosion of steel ball than Al2O3 caused by the IL.

Apparently, the best friction-reduction and anti-wear performance were obtained by IL-modified CQDs. The results might be that the CQDs are well modified by IL and unique sphere-in-shell structure of IL/CQDs is formed. The specific structure of nanoparticles provide easier shear, sliding and rolling effect between the two friction surfaces, thus the friction and wear rate significantly reduced [26]. However, with the increasing of CQDs content, a large amount of CQDs block would be formed among modified nanoparticles, which can lead to the increase of friction and wear rate. In general, IL-modified CQDs could be an excellent lubricant with the outstanding tribological performance.

In this study, we successfully fabricated nanoparticles by IL-modified CQDs. The results from FTIR and Raman indicate some functional groups are attached to the CQDs surface during functionalization. What’s more, the tribological experiment showed excellent tribological performance, and super-low friction coefficient (0.006) and wear rate (0.7×10−14 m3/Nm) are obtained with the CQDs content of 3.6%. The sphere-in-shell structure of the nanoparticles and the correlative tribological mechanism of IL/CQDs still required further investigation, and this lubricant material might be explored for other applications such as nano-electronic devices, conductivity and fluorescence techniques.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was supported by Development (973) Program of China (Grant No. 2013CB632300) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51475447).
